messages.content:
summarize the following:

The Biden administration is preparing to restrict Chinese companies' access to U.S. cloud-computing services, the Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the situation.

The new rule, if adopted, would likely require U.S. cloud-service providers such as Amazon and Microsoft to seek U.S. government permission before they provide cloud-computing services that use advanced artificial-intelligence chips to Chinese customers, the newspaper said.

The U.S. Department of Commerce is expected to implement the restriction in coming weeks as part of an expansion of its semiconductor export control policy introduced in October, the it said.

The Commerce Department, Microsoft and Amazon did not immediately respond to a request for comment outside business hours.

China said on Monday it would control exports of some metals widely used in the semiconductor industry, its latest response in an escalating dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips.

The controls on metals, which China said were aimed at protecting national security and its interests, will require exporters to seek permission to ship some gallium and germanium products.

The Biden administration is planning to limit Chinese companies' access to U.S. cloud-computing services. This would require U.S. cloud-service providers like Amazon and Microsoft to obtain permission from the U.S. government before offering these services to Chinese customers that use advanced artificial intelligence chips. The new rule is expected to be implemented by the U.S. Department of Commerce in the coming weeks as an expansion of its existing semiconductor export control policy. China has also announced controls on the export of metals used in the semiconductor industry in response to the ongoing dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips.

summarize the following:

China has imposed export curbs on two metals used in computer chips and solar cells, expanding a squabble with Washington over high-tech trade ahead of Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen’s visit to Beijing this week.
The controls on gallium and germanium are intended to “safeguard national security,” the Commerce Ministry said late Monday. It said exports will require official permission once the rules take effect Aug. 1 but did not say what restrictions might be applied.

Chinese leader Xi Jinping’s government is frustrated by U.S. curbs on access to advanced processor chips and other technology on security grounds. But Beijing has been slow to retaliate, possibly to avoid disrupting China’s fledgling developers of chips, artificial intelligence and other technology.
Yellen is due to arrive Thursday as part of efforts by the Biden administration to revive relations that have plunged to their lowest level in decades due to disputes about technology, China’s military buildup, human rights and other irritants.
China is the biggest global source of gallium and germanium, which are produced in small amounts but are needed to make computer chips for mobile phones, cars and other products, as well as solar panels and military technology.

The United States gets about half its supply of both metals directly from China, according to the U.S. Geological Survey. China exported about 23 metric tons (25 tons) of gallium last year and produces about 600 metric tons (660 tons) of germanium per year.
The United States has blocked Chinese access to advanced chips and the technology to manufacture them and has persuaded allies the Netherlands and Japan to limit exports of chipmaking tools.
That threatens to delay or derail the ruling Communist Party’s ambitions to make China more prosperous and increase its global influence by becoming a leader in clean energy, telecoms, artificial intelligence and other technologies.
In May, Beijing banned use of products from Micron Inc., the biggest U.S. producer of memory chips, in computers and network equipment deemed sensitive.

China has imposed export restrictions on gallium and germanium, two metals used in computer chips and solar cells, as part of efforts to safeguard national security. The controls will require official permission for exports starting from August 1, although specific restrictions have not been specified. This move comes amid tensions with the U.S. over access to advanced processor chips and technology. China is the largest global source of gallium and germanium, with the U.S. relying on China for about half of its supply. The U.S. has also restricted Chinese access to advanced chips and chipmaking technology, threatening China's ambitions in clean energy, telecoms, and artificial intelligence sectors.

modify "The Biden administration is planning to limit Chinese companies’ access to U.S. cloud-computing services by requiring U.S. cloud-service providers like Amazon and Microsoft to obtain permission from the U.S. government before offering these services to Chinese customers that use advanced AI chips. The new rule is expected to be implemented in the coming weeks as an expansion of its existing semiconductor export control policy. China has also announced controls on the export of metals used in the semiconductor industry, in response to the ongoing dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips. Exports of the gallium and germanium, two metal used in computer chips and solar cells, will require official permission starting from August 1, although details have not been specified. China is the largest global source of gallium and germanium, with the U.S. relying on China for about half of its supply."

The Biden administration is set to restrict Chinese companies' access to U.S. cloud-computing services. This will involve U.S. cloud-service providers like Amazon and Microsoft needing approval from the U.S. government before offering these services to Chinese customers using advanced AI chips. The rule is expected to be implemented soon as an expansion of the existing semiconductor export control policy. In response to the ongoing dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips, China has also announced controls on the export of gallium and germanium, metals vital for computer chips and solar cells. Starting from August 1, exporters will require official permission, although specific details have not been disclosed. China is the world's main source of gallium and germanium, and the U.S. depends on China for roughly half of its supply.

give a title to "The Biden administration is planning to limit Chinese companies’ access to U.S. cloud-computing services by requiring U.S. cloud-service providers like Amazon and Microsoft to obtain permission from the U.S. government before offering these services to Chinese customers that use advanced AI chips. The new rule is expected to be implemented in the coming weeks as an expansion of its existing semiconductor export control policy. In response to the ongoing dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips, China has also announced controls on the export of metals used in the semiconductor industry. Exports of the gallium and germanium, two metal used in computer chips and solar cells, will require official permission starting from August 1, although details have not been specified. China is the largest global source of gallium and germanium, with the U.S. relying on China for about half of its supply."
"US Plans to Restrict Chinese Access to Cloud-Computing Services, China Implements Controls on Semiconductor Metals"
give an alternative title
"Biden Administration Implements Restrictions on Chinese Access to US Cloud Services; China Imposes Control on Semiconductor Metal Exports"
combine the the sentences
"Biden Administration Limits Chinese Access to US Cloud Services as China Imposes Controls on Semiconductor Metal Exports."
alternative
"US Restricts Chinese Access to Cloud Services While China Controls Semiconductor Metal Exports."

summarize the following:

Inflation in Britain is proving more persistent than anticipated but that does not mean the policies deployed to tackle it are wrong, Prime Minister Rishi Sunak said on Tuesday.

"They're the right ones," he told a committee of lawmakers. "Whether that's monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy or supply side reform, that's the right toolkit that you need to deploy bringing inflation down."

UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak stated that while inflation in Britain is lasting longer than expected, the policies being implemented to address it are still appropriate. He emphasized the importance of monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and supply-side reforms as the necessary tools to tackle inflation effectively.

summarize the following: United Kingdom Prime Minister Rishi Sunak underscored on Tuesday the government will continue in its efforts to bring the prices down, pointing out that the inflation rate in the country is proving to be more persistant than expected.

However, the prime minister stressed that the measures used to tame inflation are not necessarily "wrong" despite the latest economic data, highlighting his support for the Bank of England's monetary approach. "I don't think anyone thinks we should return to a world where the government is setting interest rates," Sunak told the Liaison Committee.

"No decisions are easy when it comes to bringing inflation down, but we have to do the things that are right and for the long term benefit of the country," the prime minister added.

UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak acknowledged that inflation in the country is proving to be more persistent than expected. However, he emphasized that the measures being taken to address inflation are not necessarily wrong, expressing support for the Bank of England's monetary approach. Sunak stated that difficult decisions need to be made to bring down inflation, but they are necessary for the long-term benefit of the country.

modify "UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak acknowledged that inflation in Britain is more persistent than expected, but emphasized that the measures being implemented to address inflation are not necessarily wrong. He also highlighted the importance of monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and supply-side reforms as the necessary tools to tackle inflation effectively."

UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak acknowledged the unexpected persistence of inflation in Britain and emphasized that the measures taken to address it are not inherently incorrect. He underscored the significance of utilizing monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and implementing supply-side reforms as crucial strategies to effectively tackle inflation.

summarize the following:

 Japanese Finance Minister Shunichi Suzuki said on Tuesday Tokyo and Washington were in close contact with each other on currency moves, but declined to reveal what was being discussed.

In a news conference, Suzuki also said he had "nothing additional to say" beyond his previous remarks warning against "sharp and one-sided" moves in the yen.

Japanese Finance Minister Shunichi Suzuki stated that Tokyo and Washington are maintaining close communication regarding currency movements but did not disclose any specifics about the discussions. During a news conference, Suzuki reiterated his previous warning against "sharp and one-sided" fluctuations in the yen and stated that he had nothing further to add on the matter.

give a title to "Japanese Finance Minister Shunichi Suzuki said on Tuesday Tokyo and Washington were in close contact with each other on currency moves, but did not disclose any details about the discussions. During a news conference, Suzuki reiterated his previous warning against “sharp and one-sided” fluctuations in the yen and stated that he had nothing further to add on the matter.
UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak acknowledged that inflation in Britain is more persistent than expected, but emphasized that the measures being implemented to address inflation are not necessarily wrong. He underscored the importance of monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and supply-side reforms as the necessary tools to tackle inflation effectively.
"
"Japanese Finance Minister in Close Communication with Washington on Currency Moves as UK Prime Minister Stresses Appropriate Measures to Tackle Inflation"
alternative
"Japanese Finance Minister Discusses Currency Moves with Washington while UK Prime Minister Addresses Persistent Inflation and Appropriate Measures"

summarize the following:

U.S. automaker Tesla (TSLA.O) and its chief Chinese rival BYD (002594.SZ) achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles in the second-quarter, China Passenger Car Association (CPCA) data showed, as a fight for market share heats up.
Tesla sold 93,680 China-made electric vehicles (EVs) in June, up 18.72% from a year earlier, when Tesla's factory in Shanghai delivered 78,906 China-made Model 3 and Model Y vehicles after the city ended a two-month COVID-19 lockdown.

The U.S. automaker sold 247,217 China-made cars over April-June, the most since it started delivering vehicles from Shanghai in early 2020, the data also showed. Tesla reported record deliveries of 466,140 globally for the second quarter.
Chinese rival BYD (002594.SZ), with its Dynasty and Ocean series of EVs and petrol-electric hybrid vehicles, saw June sales surge 88.16% year-on-year to 251,685 vehicles, according to CPCA data.
This was also the first time BYD's monthly sales surpassed 250,000 units and meant that between April to June, BYD delivered 700,244 vehicles, the data showed.
CPCA estimated total sales of new energy passenger vehicles - both at home and for export, and including pure electric and plug-in hybrids - at 740,000 units in June, up 30% from a year earlier. It did not separate domestic sales and exports.
The companies' sales surge point to how they are so far managing to stay ahead of their competition even as overall auto sales slow in China amid a softening economy.
Tesla slashed prices for its two ageing models at the beginning of the year to boost sales and started a price war, prompting competitors - including BYD - to offer discounts or new lower-priced models.
The EV sector is also being given additional support from the Chinese government, which wants to drive sales to shore up a faltering economic recovery.
In June, Beijing announced 520 billion yuan ($71.67 billion) worth of purchase tax breaks on new-energy vehicles through the end of 2027. Caps on the tax exemption, however, may help drive sales growth of cheaper models that are mainly produced by domestic firms rather than premium vehicles from foreign makers, analysts said.

Data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA) showed that both Tesla and its Chinese rival, BYD, achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles for the second quarter. Tesla sold 93,680 China-made electric vehicles (EVs) in June, marking an 18.72% increase compared to the previous year. Overall, Tesla sold 247,217 China-made cars in Q2, the highest since it began delivering vehicles from its Shanghai factory. Meanwhile, BYD saw a significant surge in sales, with June sales increasing by 88.16% year-on-year to 251,685 vehicles. This strong performance indicates that both companies are maintaining a competitive edge even as the overall auto sales in China slow down. The Chinese government's support for the EV sector, such as tax breaks on new-energy vehicles, is also contributing to the sales growth.

summarize the following:

Tesla Inc.’s China output increased almost 20% in June, contributing to the company’s record quarterly sales.

The US electric-vehicle maker led by Elon Musk shipped a total of 93,680 cars from its factory in Shanghai last month, preliminary data from China’s Passenger Car Association showed on Tuesday. That compares with 78,906 units in June 2022, and 77,695 vehicles in May.  

While PCA didn’t break out local deliveries and exports, Tesla typically focuses more on the domestic market in the last month of each quarter. 

The electric-car manufacturer gained momentum at the start of the year when it slashed prices of its locally built Model 3 sedans and Model Y sport utility vehicles. It quickly faced pressure from local competitors, which followed suit with discounts. After a price war leading into the Shanghai auto show in April, deliveries started to pick up again and the overall market for new-energy vehicles remained strong. 

China’s total sales of new-energy passenger vehicles to dealers in June rose an estimated 30% from a year ago, and were up 10% from May, to 740,000 units, PCA said. The government last month announced an extension of tax breaks for consumers buying clean cars through 2027, its latest move to boost sales and production in the world’s biggest EV market.

Preliminary data from China's Passenger Car Association (PCA) revealed that Tesla's China output increased by nearly 20% in June, contributing to the company's record quarterly sales. In total, Tesla shipped 93,680 cars from its Shanghai factory last month, compared to 78,906 units in June 2022 and 77,695 vehicles in May. Although the PCA did not provide a breakdown of local deliveries versus exports, Tesla generally focuses more on the domestic market in the final month of each quarter. The company's momentum was bolstered by price reductions for its locally built Model 3 sedans and Model Y SUVs earlier in the year, prompting local competitors to adopt similar discounts. Following a price war leading up to the Shanghai auto show in April, deliveries rebounded, and the demand for new-energy vehicles in the Chinese market remained robust. The PCA estimated that China's total sales of new-energy passenger vehicles to dealers increased by approximately 30% year-on-year and 10% month-on-month in June, reaching 740,000 units. The Chinese government's recent extension of tax breaks for clean cars until 2027 further aims to stimulate sales and production in the world's largest electric vehicle market.

shorten "Data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA) showed that both Tesla and its Chinese rival, BYD, achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles for the second quarter. Tesla sold 93,680 China-made electric vehicles (EVs) in June, marking an 18.72% increase compared to the previous year. Overall, Tesla sold 247,217 China-made cars in Q2, the highest since it began delivering vehicles from its Shanghai factory. Meanwhile, BYD saw a significant surge in sales, with June sales increasing by 88.16% year-on-year to 251,685 vehicles. This strong performance indicates that both companies are maintaining a competitive edge even as the overall auto sales in China slow down. In June, Beijing announced 520 billion yuan ($71.67 billion) worth of purchase tax breaks on new-energy vehicles until 2027 to further stimulate sales and production of EV in the country. "

Tesla and BYD achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles in Q2, with Tesla selling 93,680 EVs in June, an 18.72% increase from the previous year. Tesla sold a total of 247,217 China-made cars in Q2, its highest quarterly figure from its Shanghai factory. BYD also experienced a significant sales surge, with June sales increasing by 88.16% YoY to 251,685 vehicles. This performance highlights their competitive edge despite the overall slowdown in auto sales in China. Beijing's announcement of tax breaks worth 520 billion yuan ($71.67 billion) until 2027 further aims to stimulate the sales and production of EVs in China.
